questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Seed Vault Room
At the state agricultural college, the most protected room is not filled with computers or expensive machinery. It is a cold storage room lined with metal shelves and sealed jars. Inside the jars are seeds from beans, corn, squash, melons, peppers, and grains that have been grown in the region for generations.
Professor Helena Ruiz manages the collection. She explains that a seed vault is not only a museum of old crops. It is a working resource. Some varieties survive heat, poor soil, short growing seasons, or local insects better than newer commercial varieties. If farmers and plant breeders lose those older seeds, they may also lose traits that could help future crops adapt to changing conditions.
The work requires more than placing seeds on a shelf. Seeds must be dried, labeled, stored at controlled temperatures, and tested regularly. If too many seeds from one variety fail to sprout during testing, the college grows a new crop from the remaining seeds and saves fresh seed for storage. This process keeps the collection alive.
Ruiz’s team also records stories from farmers who donate seeds. A jar label may list a variety name and date, but the farmer may know that the seed came from a grandparent’s garden or survived a harsh drought decades ago. The vault protects both biological material and practical memory.
The cold room seems quiet, but it holds choices for the future. Each jar is a small reserve of possibility.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A seed vault preserves older crop varieties and the useful traits and stories connected to them.
choices.B: A seed vault is mainly a display room for farm tools and machines.
choices.C: The college stores seeds only because newer commercial varieties cannot grow anywhere.
choices.D: Professor Ruiz believes seeds should never be tested after storage.

questions.correctAnswerText: A seed vault preserves older crop varieties and the useful traits and stories connected to them.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the vault protects crop traits, seed viability, and farmer knowledge connected to older varieties.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seed Vault Room.”
Why can older seed varieties be valuable for future crops?

choices.A: They may contain traits that help crops survive heat, poor soil, insects, or short growing seasons.
choices.B: They always grow faster than every modern crop variety.
choices.C: They are stored in jars that never need labels.
choices.D: They replace the need for farmers to plant crops.

questions.correctAnswerText: They may contain traits that help crops survive heat, poor soil, insects, or short growing seasons.
questions.explanation: The passage states that older varieties may have traits that help future crops adapt.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Seed Vault Room.”
Which TWO tasks help keep the seed collection useful?

choices.A: Testing whether stored seeds still sprout
choices.B: Saving fresh seed when older samples weaken
choices.C: Mixing all seeds together in one container
choices.D: Storing seeds at random room temperatures

questions.correctAnswerText: Testing whether stored seeds still sprout | Saving fresh seed when older samples weaken
questions.explanation: Testing and renewing seeds help keep the collection alive and useful.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seed Vault Room.”
What can be inferred from the college’s practice of recording farmers’ stories?

choices.A: The college values the human knowledge connected to seed varieties.
choices.B: The college cares only about the price of seeds.
choices.C: Farmers are not allowed to explain where seeds came from.
choices.D: Seed labels are considered useless by the college.

questions.correctAnswerText: The college values the human knowledge connected to seed varieties.
questions.explanation: Recording stories shows that the collection includes practical memory, not only biological material.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seed Vault Room.”
In the sentence “Each jar is a small reserve of possibility,” reserve most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=supply kept for future use
questions.explanation: In context, reserve means something saved for possible future need.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Seed Vault Room.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Some older varieties survive heat, poor soil, or local insects.
tiles.B: Seeds are dried, labeled, stored, and tested regularly.
tiles.C: Farmer stories may explain that a seed survived a harsh drought decades ago.
tiles.D: The college keeps the cold room open to any visitor without records.

targets.T1: Evidence that older seeds may have useful traits
targets.T2: Evidence that preservation requires careful maintenance
targets.T3: Evidence that the collection includes practical memory

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Some older varieties survive heat, poor soil, or local insects.; T2=Seeds are dried, labeled, stored, and tested regularly.; T3=Farmer stories may explain that a seed survived a harsh drought decades ago.
questions.explanation: The tiles match useful traits, careful maintenance, and practical memory.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Time Clock Correction Procedure
To: Hourly Employees, Shift Leads, and Payroll Assistants
From: Payroll Operations Manager
Subject: Time Clock Correction Procedure
Beginning October 6, employees who forget to clock in or out must submit a Time Clock Correction Form before the end of the next scheduled shift. This procedure applies to missed punches, incorrect department codes, meal-break errors, and accidental duplicate punches.
Employees must complete the form with the work date, scheduled shift, actual start or end time, reason for the correction, and employee signature. A shift lead must review the request and confirm that the corrected time matches the schedule, assignment records, or other available information. Employees should not ask coworkers to clock in or out for them under any circumstances.
Payroll assistants will process approved corrections on Monday and Thursday mornings. If a form is incomplete or does not include shift lead approval, it will be returned to the employee for correction. Repeated missed punches may require a meeting with a supervisor to review timekeeping expectations.
This procedure is intended to keep pay records accurate and prevent confusion about hours worked. It is not meant to punish an employee for one honest mistake. Accurate time records help employees receive correct pay and help departments track staffing needs.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how employees should request corrections to time clock records
choices.B: To allow coworkers to clock in for each other when shifts are busy
choices.C: To cancel payroll processing on Mondays and Thursdays
choices.D: To require employees to ignore missed punches

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how employees should request corrections to time clock records
questions.explanation: The document explains the process for missed punches, duplicate punches, and other time record corrections.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Time Clock Correction Procedure.”
When must an employee submit a Time Clock Correction Form?

choices.A: Before the end of the next scheduled shift
choices.B: Only at the end of the year
choices.C: Before the employee is hired
choices.D: After payroll has ignored the correction twice

questions.correctAnswerText: Before the end of the next scheduled shift
questions.explanation: The procedure says employees must submit the form by that deadline.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Time Clock Correction Procedure.”
Which TWO details must be included on the correction form?

choices.A: The work date
choices.B: The reason for the correction
choices.C: The employee’s favorite shift
choices.D: The customer’s phone number

questions.correctAnswerText: The work date | The reason for the correction
questions.explanation: The form must include the work date, reason for correction, and other details.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Time Clock Correction Procedure.”
Why must a shift lead review the correction request?

choices.A: To confirm that the corrected time matches available work records
choices.B: To decide whether payroll assistants should stop processing corrections
choices.C: To remove the employee’s signature from the form
choices.D: To allow a coworker to clock in for the employee

questions.correctAnswerText: To confirm that the corrected time matches available work records
questions.explanation: Shift lead review helps verify that the correction is accurate.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Time Clock Correction Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning October 6, employees who forget to clock in or out must submit a Time Clock Correction Form.”
choices.B: “Payroll assistants will process approved corrections on Monday and Thursday mornings.”
choices.C: “This procedure is intended to keep pay records accurate and prevent confusion about hours worked.”
choices.D: “Repeated missed punches may require a meeting with a supervisor.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “This procedure is intended to keep pay records accurate and prevent confusion about hours worked.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the purpose of the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Use Move-In Condition Checklists
Landlords and tenants should complete a move-in condition checklist before a renter takes possession of an apartment. The checklist should describe the condition of floors, walls, appliances, windows, locks, plumbing fixtures, smoke alarms, and any furniture included with the rental. Both sides should sign it and keep a copy.
A checklist protects tenants by creating a record of existing damage. Without one, a renter may later be charged for a cracked tile, stained carpet, or broken blind that was already present on move-in day. Photos can help, but a written checklist signed by both sides makes the record clearer.
The checklist also protects landlords. If damage occurs during the lease, the landlord has a baseline for comparison. This can reduce arguments when the tenant moves out and the security deposit is reviewed. Clear documentation helps both sides focus on facts instead of memory.
Some people may say that a checklist adds paperwork to an already busy moving day. That is true, but the task does not need to be complicated. A simple room-by-room form completed before boxes are unpacked can prevent larger disputes later. Moving into a rental is a major financial commitment, and a careful record at the beginning is a reasonable step.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Move-in condition checklists should be used because they create a clear record for tenants and landlords.
choices.B: Tenants should be charged for all apartment damage, even if it existed before move-in.
choices.C: Landlords should never inspect rental units before a lease begins.
choices.D: Photos and written records should be banned during moving day.

questions.correctAnswerText: Move-in condition checklists should be used because they create a clear record for tenants and landlords.
questions.explanation: The author argues that checklists protect both sides by documenting apartment condition at the start.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Move-In Condition Checklists.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that checklists protect tenants?

choices.A: A renter may be charged for damage that was already present if no record exists.
choices.B: Moving day can be busy.
choices.C: A landlord may compare apartment condition at move-out.
choices.D: Both sides should keep a copy.

questions.correctAnswerText: A renter may be charged for damage that was already present if no record exists.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the claim that checklists protect tenants from unfair charges.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Use Move-In Condition Checklists.”
Which TWO apartment features does the author say should be described on the checklist?

choices.A: Floors
choices.B: Smoke alarms
choices.C: The tenant’s grocery list
choices.D: The landlord’s favorite neighborhood

questions.correctAnswerText: Floors | Smoke alarms
questions.explanation: The passage lists floors and smoke alarms among items to describe.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Move-In Condition Checklists.”
How does the author respond to the concern that the checklist adds paperwork?

choices.A: By arguing that a simple form can prevent larger disputes later
choices.B: By saying paperwork is always more important than moving
choices.C: By claiming moving day is never busy
choices.D: By recommending that tenants sign a blank checklist

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that a simple form can prevent larger disputes later
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges the paperwork concern and explains why the checklist is still worthwhile.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Move-In Condition Checklists.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 3: [D1]

D1: A tenant mediation office reported that many deposit disputes involved disagreement about whether damage existed before move-in.
The checklist can be printed on white paper.
Some tenants prefer to move on weekends.

questions.correct: D1=A tenant mediation office reported that many deposit disputes involved disagreement about whether damage existed before move-in.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A tenant mediation office reported that many deposit disputes involved disagreement about whether damage existed before move-in.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by showing that move-in condition disputes are common.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Move-In Condition Checklists.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Written records made at move-in can help settle later disagreements about apartment condition.
choices.B: Tenants and landlords always remember every detail perfectly.
choices.C: Security deposits are never affected by apartment damage.
choices.D: Apartments contain no features that can be inspected before move-in.

questions.correctAnswerText: Written records made at move-in can help settle later disagreements about apartment condition.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that early documentation can clarify later disputes.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Blue Glaze
Mina had swept the pottery studio for a year before she was allowed to prepare glaze for the community class. Usually, she stacked clay blocks, cleaned wheels, and carried dry bowls to the storage shelf. Mixing glaze belonged to Mr. Vale, who could glance into a bucket and know whether the color would dry flat, glossy, pale, or deep.
On Wednesday, he placed a notebook beside the sink. “Blue glaze today,” he said. “Follow the ratios. Stir slowly. Strain twice.”
Mina read the recipe three times. The powders looked almost identical in their bags, but the notebook gave each one a weight and order. She measured carefully, afraid that one extra scoop would turn the students’ bowls the wrong color. When she added water, the mixture looked gray and disappointing.
“It always looks like that now,” Mr. Vale said. “The kiln tells the truth later.”
Mina stirred until her shoulder ached, then poured the glaze through a fine screen. Small lumps remained, so she strained it again. By evening, the students dipped their bowls into the bucket she had prepared.
Two days later, the kiln cooled enough to open. The first bowl came out blue as rainwater in a barrel. It was not Mina’s bowl, and no one would know she had mixed the glaze unless she told them. Still, when the students lifted their finished pieces, Mina felt as if a little of the color belonged to her.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Mina must trust a careful process while taking on a new responsibility in the pottery studio.
choices.B: Mina tries to convince Mr. Vale to stop teaching pottery classes.
choices.C: Mina refuses to follow the glaze recipe.
choices.D: The students decide not to use the glaze Mina prepared.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mina must trust a careful process while taking on a new responsibility in the pottery studio.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Mina following the glaze process despite uncertainty about the result.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Blue Glaze.”
Why does Mina read the recipe three times?

choices.A: She wants to avoid making a mistake with an unfamiliar responsibility.
choices.B: She cannot find the notebook beside the sink.
choices.C: She wants to change the ratios before class.
choices.D: She is trying to memorize a student’s name.

questions.correctAnswerText: She wants to avoid making a mistake with an unfamiliar responsibility.
questions.explanation: Mina reads the recipe repeatedly because the task is new and important.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Blue Glaze.”
What does Mr. Vale mean when he says, “The kiln tells the truth later”?

choices.A: The final color will appear only after the glazed pieces are fired.
choices.B: The kiln will decide which students may attend class.
choices.C: The glaze should be judged only by how it looks before firing.
choices.D: The notebook does not matter after water is added.

questions.correctAnswerText: The final color will appear only after the glazed pieces are fired.
questions.explanation: Mr. Vale explains that the glaze’s current gray appearance does not reveal the final result.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Blue Glaze.”
Which TWO details show Mina working carefully?

choices.A: She measures the powders according to the notebook.
choices.B: She strains the glaze a second time when lumps remain.
choices.C: She ignores the order listed in the recipe.
choices.D: She leaves the mixture unstirred.
choices.E: She decides the gray color means the glaze has failed.

questions.correctAnswerText: She measures the powders according to the notebook. | She strains the glaze a second time when lumps remain.
questions.explanation: These details show that Mina follows the process carefully.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Blue Glaze.”
What does the final sentence suggest about Mina?

choices.A: She feels pride in work that contributed to others’ finished pieces.
choices.B: She believes only her own pottery should be fired.
choices.C: She is disappointed that the bowls turned blue.
choices.D: She plans to stop helping in the studio.

questions.correctAnswerText: She feels pride in work that contributed to others’ finished pieces.
questions.explanation: Mina did not make the bowls, but her careful work helped create their final color.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Blue Glaze.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Mina reads the recipe three times.
tiles.B: Mr. Vale tells Mina the glaze will look different after firing.
tiles.C: The first bowl comes out blue as rainwater in a barrel.
tiles.D: Mina throws away the glaze before class.

targets.T2: Guidance that helps Mina wait for the process

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Mina reads the recipe three times.; T2=Mr. Vale tells Mina the glaze will look different after firing.; T3=The first bowl comes out blue as rainwater in a barrel.
questions.explanation: The tiles show Mina’s caution, guidance about the process, and the successful result.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Medical Prior Authorization Notice
A medical prior authorization notice explains whether a health plan has approved a requested service, test, prescription, or medical device before it is provided. Some insurance plans require this review for certain treatments to confirm that the service meets plan rules. The notice can be confusing, but reading it carefully helps patients understand what has been approved, denied, or still needs more information.
One important detail is the status of the request. An approval means the plan has agreed that the service meets its requirements, though normal costs may still apply. A denial means the plan has not approved the request as submitted. A pending notice means more records, test results, or provider information may be needed before a decision is made.
Patients should also read the reason section. A denial may not mean the treatment is medically useless. It may mean the plan requires a different step first, needs more documentation, or considers the request outside its rules. The notice should explain appeal rights or next steps.
A prior authorization notice is not the same as a medical opinion from a doctor. It is part of the insurance review process. Understanding the notice can help patients ask clearer questions of both the health plan and the medical office.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A prior authorization notice helps patients understand insurance review decisions and possible next steps.
choices.B: A prior authorization notice is always a doctor’s final medical opinion.
choices.C: A denial always means a treatment is medically useless.
choices.D: Patients should ignore pending notices because no action may be needed.

questions.correctAnswerText: A prior authorization notice helps patients understand insurance review decisions and possible next steps.
questions.explanation: The passage explains approval, denial, pending status, reasons, and next steps.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Medical Prior Authorization Notice.”
What does a pending notice usually mean?

choices.A: More information may be needed before a decision is made.
choices.B: The service has been permanently canceled by the patient.
choices.C: The doctor has changed the diagnosis.
choices.D: Normal costs no longer apply to any service.

questions.correctAnswerText: More information may be needed before a decision is made.
questions.explanation: A pending notice means the plan may need more records or provider information.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Medical Prior Authorization Notice.”
Why should patients read the reason section of a denial?

choices.A: It may explain whether more documentation, a different step, or an appeal is needed.
choices.B: It always proves that the doctor made a mistake.
choices.C: It replaces all conversations with the medical office.
choices.D: It shows that the health plan has approved every future request.

questions.correctAnswerText: It may explain whether more documentation, a different step, or an appeal is needed.
questions.explanation: The reason section helps patients understand why the request was denied and what can happen next.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Medical Prior Authorization Notice.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that insurance review is different from medical advice?

choices.A: “Some insurance plans require this review for certain treatments.”
choices.B: “An approval means the plan has agreed that the service meets its requirements.”
choices.C: “A prior authorization notice is not the same as a medical opinion from a doctor.”
choices.D: “The notice can be confusing, but reading it carefully helps patients understand.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A prior authorization notice is not the same as a medical opinion from a doctor.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly supports the distinction between insurance review and medical advice.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Medical Prior Authorization Notice.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It defines prior authorization notices, explains possible statuses, and advises patients to review reasons and next steps.
choices.B: It tells a story about one patient changing doctors.
choices.C: It compares hospitals by the number of tests they provide.
choices.D: It argues that patients should never contact health plans.

questions.correctAnswerText: It defines prior authorization notices, explains possible statuses, and advises patients to review reasons and next steps.
questions.explanation: The passage is organized by definition, status meanings, and practical advice.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Certification Renewal Reminder
(1) Employees whose safety certifications expire this quarter must submit renewal documents by November 15. (2) The packet should include the renewal form, proof of completed training, and a copy of the current certification card. (3) Supervisors review the documents before forwarding them to compliance. (4) [D1], incomplete packets may delay an employee’s assignment to certain projects. (5) The approved packet [D2] filed by the compliance coordinator. (6) Employees should keep a copy of all renewal documents.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “Therefore” links incomplete packets to possible delays, and “will be filed” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Certification Renewal Reminder.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Employees should keep a copy of all renewal documents.
choices.B: Employees should keep, for themselves, a copy of documents that are for renewal.
choices.C: A copy of every renewal document should be kept by employees for keeping.
choices.D: Employees, if they have documents for renewal, should keep copies of those documents.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employees should keep a copy of all renewal documents.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and preserves the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The packets on the compliance desk needs review.
choices.B: The list of certification dates are posted near the office.
choices.C: The employees who renew certifications receive confirmation emails.
choices.D: The folder of training records have been updated.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employees who renew certifications receive confirmation emails.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “employees” correctly takes the plural verb “receive.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Harper called Quinn after the training, she updated the renewal form.
choices.B: Harper called Quinn after the training, and Harper updated the renewal form.
choices.C: She updated it when Harper called Quinn after the training.
choices.D: After the training, she called her and updated it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Harper called Quinn after the training, and Harper updated the renewal form.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Harper” makes clear who updated the form.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The training provider changed the certificate format. [D1], compliance accepted both the old and new versions this month.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=However
questions.explanation: “However” signals contrast between the changed format and the temporary acceptance of both versions.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The packet was complete: compliance filed it the same day.
choices.B: The packet was complete compliance filed it the same day.
choices.C: The packet was complete, compliance filed it the same day.
choices.D: The packet was complete: and compliance, filed it the same day.

questions.correctAnswerText: The packet was complete: compliance filed it the same day.
questions.explanation: The colon correctly connects a complete sentence to a closely related result.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the certification card was expired.
choices.B: After the supervisor reviewed the packet.
choices.C: Compliance filed the approved renewal documents.
choices.D: While checking the training dates.

questions.correctAnswerText: Compliance filed the approved renewal documents.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The supervisor reviewed the packet and forwards it yesterday.
choices.B: The supervisor reviews the packet and forwarded it yesterday.
choices.C: The supervisor reviewed the packet and forwarded it yesterday.
choices.D: The supervisor had reviewed the packet and forwards it yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supervisor reviewed the packet and forwarded it yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The packet includes a renewal form, training proof, and whether the card is current.
choices.B: The packet includes a renewal form, proof of training, and a current certification card.
choices.C: The packet includes renewing the form, proof of training, and to copy the card.
choices.D: The packet includes a form that is renewed, training proof, and if the card is current.

questions.correctAnswerText: The packet includes a renewal form, proof of training, and a current certification card.
questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Cross-Train Employees for Key Roles
A distribution company should cross-train employees for several key roles. When only one person knows how to complete a task, a sick day, vacation, or resignation can interrupt the entire workflow. Cross-training gives employees basic ability in related tasks so that the department can continue operating when someone is absent.
Cross-training can also improve problem solving. An employee who understands how receiving, order picking, inventory updates, and shipping connect may make better decisions in their own role. Workers can see how an error in one area affects another. This broader understanding can reduce blame and encourage cooperation.
The program should be organized carefully. Employees do not need to master every job at once. Managers can identify the most important backup tasks, create short practice sessions, and document who is qualified to cover each role. Cross-training is not about making every employee do more work for the same pay. It is about building flexibility and reducing disruption.
Passage B: Keep Roles Specialized
A distribution company should keep most roles specialized. Employees become faster and more accurate when they focus on specific tasks. A worker who spends most of the day processing receiving paperwork may develop attention to details that a briefly trained backup person would miss. In a busy operation, small errors can create shipment delays and inventory problems.
Cross-training can also create confusion. If employees rotate too often, they may not know which role takes priority or who is responsible for final decisions. Managers may spend extra time correcting mistakes from people who are only partly trained. Instead of spreading workers across many tasks, the company should improve backup planning within each specialty.
Specialization does not mean that no one else can help during an absence. The company can assign a trained backup for each critical role, but that backup should receive deeper instruction rather than quick exposure to many tasks. Reliable operations depend on skill, not just flexibility.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports broad cross-training for flexibility, while Passage B argues specialized roles protect accuracy.
choices.B: Passage A argues that employees should work without training, while Passage B argues that backups are never needed.
choices.C: Passage A supports closing the distribution company, while Passage B supports eliminating all records.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on seed storage, while Passage B focuses on medical notices.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports broad cross-training for flexibility, while Passage B argues specialized roles protect accuracy.
questions.explanation: Passage A emphasizes coverage and cooperation; Passage B emphasizes accuracy and deep task knowledge.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about cross-training.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: When only one person knows a task, an absence can interrupt the workflow.
choices.B: Specialized workers may notice details that backups miss.
choices.C: Cross-training can create confusion if employees rotate too often.
choices.D: Small errors can create shipment delays.

questions.correctAnswerText: When only one person knows a task, an absence can interrupt the workflow.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s argument for cross-training.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about cross-training.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Specialized employees may become faster and more accurate.
choices.B: Partly trained workers may make mistakes that managers must correct.
choices.C: Cross-training always prevents every inventory problem.
choices.D: Employees should never understand how departments connect.
choices.E: Backup planning is unnecessary in busy operations.

questions.correctAnswerText: Specialized employees may become faster and more accurate. | Partly trained workers may make mistakes that managers must correct.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims as reasons to preserve specialization.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about cross-training.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses concern about overwhelming employees?

choices.A: “The company should cross-train employees for several key roles.”
choices.B: “Employees do not need to master every job at once.”
choices.C: “Workers can see how an error in one area affects another.”
choices.D: “Cross-training gives employees basic ability in related tasks.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Employees do not need to master every job at once.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly addresses the concern that cross-training might overwhelm employees.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about cross-training.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors want reliable operations, but they disagree about whether broad flexibility or specialization is the better path.
choices.B: Both authors believe employee training is unnecessary.
choices.C: Both authors think absences never affect distribution work.
choices.D: Both authors argue that managers should stop documenting qualifications.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors want reliable operations, but they disagree about whether broad flexibility or specialization is the better path.
questions.explanation: Both passages focus on operational reliability but recommend different methods.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about cross-training.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on absence coverage, error rates, training time, workflow interruptions, and employee performance after cross-training
choices.B: A list of break room decorations
choices.C: A history of delivery truck colors
choices.D: A survey asking employees which parking row they prefer

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on absence coverage, error rates, training time, workflow interruptions, and employee performance after cross-training
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate the benefits and risks of cross-training.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Filed in the compliance cabinet, Mateo reviewed the renewal packets before lunch.

choices.A: Filed in the compliance cabinet, Mateo reviewed the renewal packets before lunch.
choices.B: Mateo reviewed the renewal packets filed in the compliance cabinet before lunch.
choices.C: Mateo, filed in the compliance cabinet, reviewed the renewal packets before lunch.
choices.D: The compliance cabinet reviewed Mateo’s renewal packets before lunch.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mateo reviewed the renewal packets filed in the compliance cabinet before lunch.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “renewal packets,” the items filed in the cabinet.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The coordinator sent a reminder. The reminder listed the renewal deadline.

choices.A: The coordinator sent a reminder that listed the renewal deadline.
choices.B: The reminder sent the coordinator and listed the renewal deadline.
choices.C: Listing the coordinator, the renewal deadline sent a reminder.
choices.D: The coordinator sent, and the renewal deadline listed the reminder.

questions.correctAnswerText: The coordinator sent a reminder that listed the renewal deadline.
questions.explanation: This sentence combines the ideas clearly without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The employee’s certification card was copied for the packet.
choices.B: The employees certification card was copied for the packet.
choices.C: The employees’ certification card’s was copied for the packet.
choices.D: The employee cards’ was copied for the packet.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employee’s certification card was copied for the packet.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the card belongs to one employee.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Written Auto Repair Estimates Before Work Begins
Auto repair shops should be required to provide written estimates before beginning non-emergency repair work. A written estimate helps customers understand the expected cost of parts, labor, fees, and taxes before they agree to the repair. Without one, a customer may approve work without realizing how expensive the final bill could become.
Written estimates also reduce disputes. If the shop later discovers additional problems, it can contact the customer for approval before going beyond the estimate. This protects the customer from surprise charges and protects the shop from claims that the customer never agreed to the work. A clear estimate creates a shared record of what was discussed.
The requirement can include reasonable flexibility. If a vehicle is unsafe to drive or must be moved quickly, emergency work may be handled differently. If the estimate cannot be exact until a part is removed, the shop can explain that in writing and give a range. The goal is not to prevent repairs. The goal is to make the agreement clear before money is spent.
Most responsible shops already communicate costs honestly. A written estimate rule would set a basic standard for all shops and help customers make informed decisions when they may already be stressed about transportation.
Passage B:
Do Not Add Strict Estimate Rules for Repair Shops
Repair shops should not face strict new rules requiring written estimates before most work begins. Repair work often changes once a mechanic can inspect the vehicle more closely. A noise that seems minor may lead to a serious problem behind a panel, under the engine, or inside a brake assembly. A written estimate created too early may give customers a false sense of certainty.
Strict estimate rules can also slow service. Some customers need their vehicles back quickly for work, school, or family responsibilities. If a shop must stop repeatedly for written approval, repairs may take longer. Small shops may also struggle with extra paperwork, especially when they already explain costs by phone or in person.
There are better ways to protect customers. Shops should explain hourly rates, ask before making major additional repairs, and provide itemized final invoices. Customers can request written estimates from shops that offer them. Reviews and repeat business already encourage honest communication.
A broad rule may treat every repair situation the same even though repairs vary widely. Good communication matters, but lawmakers should avoid requirements that create delays and unrealistic expectations.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether auto repair shops should be required to provide written estimates before non-emergency work begins? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
